unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Unit 7 follows Amal, Nora and Teacher Yasmin from the coast to the forest and up a mountain trail. Learners meet fifteen nature words, from climate and temperature to matter, energy and planet. They use the present perfect to say what they have seen, explored and visited, ask “Have you ever…?” questions, and build describing phrases such as “a metal rock on the beach”. The unit closes with clear instructions and a short poem about caring for our planet.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary section and learn a few nature words each day.
Work through the six grammar lessons, one at a time — never all at once. Lesson 6 has four parts, so take it slowly.
Read “Our Wonderful Nature”, the poem, the two listening texts and the story “From Coast to Forest”.
Answer the comprehension questions, then move through Speaking, Writing and Activities.
Finish with the quiz, the unit assignment and the self-assessment.

visual.alt: Illustrated children on a nature trip, looking at a beach, a forest and a mountain

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The usual weather in a place over a long time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The desert has a hot, dry climate.
practiceSentences: Our island climate stays warm from January to December.
A rainy climate keeps the hills green all year.
Does the climate change as you climb a mountain?
The teacher said, "Compare the desert climate with the coast."
I like our warm climate for playing outside.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - i - m - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting beside me, ask me how climate is different from today's weather, then help me name two places that have a hot, dry climate.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: What the sky and air are like today.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is sunny and warm.
practiceSentences: The weather changed from bright sunshine to heavy rain.
Stormy weather kept the fishing boats at home.
Will the weather be dry for sports day?
Sami said, "I recorded the weather all week."
My favourite weather is cool wind after rain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - e - a - t - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper listening, ask me to describe the weather outside our window in two sentences, then check that I used a describing word.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How hot or cold something is.
practiceSentences: We check the temperature before we leave for school.
The temperature rises quickly in the desert at noon.
Does the temperature fall on the mountain top?
Maya said, "The temperature reached forty degrees today."
I wrote the morning temperature on our class chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - m - p - e - r - a - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to listen while I say temperature slowly, syllable by syllable, then ask me to read three temperatures from a weather chart.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The light and warmth that come from the sun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sunshine helped the flowers grow.
practiceSentences: Warm sunshine dried the wet towels on the line.
Bright sunshine turned the puddles into little mirrors.
Do tomatoes need sunshine every day to grow?
Theo said, "Sunshine after rain makes a rainbow."
I sat in the sunshine and read my book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - n - s - h - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me to name three things that sunshine helps to do, and help me put one of them into a full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Turned to ice because it was very cold. (past of freeze)
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The water in the pond froze in the cold night.
practiceSentences: The pond froze so hard that the ducks walked across.
The puddles froze into slippery glass overnight.
Do you know why the mountain lake froze?
Nora said, "My juice froze into a tasty ice lolly."
My fingers felt like they froze in the cold wind.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me, while an adult listens, to say the three forms freeze, froze and frozen, then give me one sentence to finish with the right form.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very tall hill with steep sides.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw white snow on top of the mountain.
practiceSentences: A narrow track winds up the side of the mountain.
The climbers rested halfway up the steep mountain.
Which mountain is the highest in the world?
Idris said, "The mountain path is rocky and narrow."
I drew a purple mountain behind the village.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - u - n - t - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult helping me, ask me how a mountain is different from a hill, then ask me to describe a mountain using two adjectives.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big area with many trees.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The deer ran through the green forest.
practiceSentences: Tall trees keep the forest cool on hot days.
Owls sleep in the shady forest all day.
What animals live deep in the forest?
Sami said, "The forest smells of rain and leaves."
I heard birdsong echo through the forest.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - o - r - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to list four things I would hear inside a forest while my adult helper writes them down, then help me build one sentence from the list.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Land with sand near the sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I built sandcastles on the beach.
practiceSentences: Our class picked up litter along the whole beach.
Tiny crabs hide under stones on the beach.
Shall we collect shells at the beach tomorrow?
Maya said, "The beach sand was warm under my feet."
I flew my kite along the windy beach.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me what I would take to the beach and why, and check that each answer is a complete sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The land next to the sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Fishermen live in a village near the coast.
practiceSentences: Strong waves crash against the rocks on the coast.
The road follows the coast past little fishing villages.
Can you see dolphins from the coast here?
Theo said, "The coast is cooler than the city."
I watched boats sail along the coast at sunset.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to explain the difference between the coast and the beach to the adult beside me, then correct me kindly if I mix them up.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The land, water, animals, and plants around us.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We took a walk to enjoy nature.
practiceSentences: Every Saturday morning we walk and study nature together.
Nature gives us shade, fruit, and fresh air.
What sounds of nature can you hear right now?
Leo said, "Nature is full of clever patterns."
I keep a nature diary of birds I see.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - a - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me to name four parts of nature I can see from our door, then help me sort them into living and non-living.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go and look at new places.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will explore the forest path tomorrow.
practiceSentences: Tomorrow we will explore the caves beside the river.
The class will explore the rock pools on the trip.
Which path shall we explore first today?
Idris said, "Let us explore the old olive grove."
I explore my grandmother's garden every visit.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - l - o - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me where I would like to explore and why, while an adult listens, then help me turn my answer into a present perfect sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Power that helps things move or work.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We use the sun for clean energy.
practiceSentences: Wind turbines turn moving air into useful energy.
The sun gives clean energy to the solar panels.
Where does a car get its energy from?
The teacher said, "Food gives your body energy."
I have lots of energy after a good sleep.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - e - r - g - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper, ask me for two ways our family could save energy at home, and help me write the better idea as one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: What everything is made of; it takes up space.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Water, rock, and air are all types of matter.
practiceSentences: Ice, water, and steam are three forms of matter.
Solid, liquid, and gas are states of matter.
Is air a kind of matter too?
Sami said, "Everything around us is made of matter."
I sorted pictures of matter into solids and liquids.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - t - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me, with an adult in the room, to point at one solid, one liquid and one gas, and to say why all three are matter.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A hard, shiny material used to make things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The spoon is made of metal.
practiceSentences: Omar sells pots made of light, strong metal.
The gate is made of strong, shiny metal.
Why does metal feel cold in the morning?
Maya said, "A magnet sticks to some metal things."
I found an old metal coin in the sand.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult helping me search safely, ask me to find one metal object at home and describe it with two describing words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big ball in space, like Earth or Mars.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Earth is the planet we live on.
practiceSentences: Our planet needs clean water, clean air, and trees.
Earth is the only planet with big blue oceans.
Which planet is famous for its rings?
Theo said, "Mars is called the red planet."
I made a model planet out of clay.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - l - a - n - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to name our planet and one job I can do to keep it clean, then let the adult beside me add a second idea.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe the beach, the coast, the forest and the mountain using unit vocabulary.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All four place words used correctly in the Speaking 1 recording and in the Writing 1 description of nature near home.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain the difference between weather, climate and temperature, and describe today's weather.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A correct weather-versus-climate explanation in Speaking 2, plus quiz items 1 and 2 answered correctly.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Form present perfect sentences with have or has and the correct past participle.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three accurate present perfect sentences in Writing 2 and the have/has gaps completed in Activity 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask “Have you ever…?” questions and reply with a correct short answer.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A recorded question-and-answer exchange in Speaking 3 in which both the question and the short answer keep the right helping verb.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Build describing phrases such as “a metal rock” to make writing clearer.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: At least four adjective-plus-noun phrases in the Writing 4 report, and two describing words added during Writing 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write imperatives and reported instructions that tell people how to care for the planet.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Two verb-first commands in Activity 4 and the numbered fact file in Writing 5, both checked for full stops.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read “From Coast to Forest” and explain what the characters felt and learned.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Comprehension questions 9 to 12 answered with a quoted detail from the story, and one section read aloud with expression.

readings.title: Our Wonderful Nature
readings.setting: A coastal town: the school, the beach and coast, a forest path and a mountain trail
readings.passageScript: Nature is all around us. The sunshine warms the land. Trees grow tall in the forest. Waves splash on the beach near the coast. High above, the mountain touches the clouds. All of this is our home — the planet Earth. We must take care of it.

readings.title: A Poem: “Nature Is Our Home”
readings.setting: A coastal town: the school, the beach and coast, a forest path and a mountain trail
readings.passageScript:
Nature is our home,
It gives us water, trees, and air.
The sun, the sea, the mountain tall —
Let's take care, and share it all.

readings.title: Getting Ready for the Trip
readings.setting: A coastal town: the school, the beach and coast, a forest path and a mountain trail
readings.passageScript: “Tomorrow we will explore the coast and the forest. Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see. And remember — protect the planet!”

readings.title: Have You Ever…?
readings.setting: A coastal town: the school, the beach and coast, a forest path and a mountain trail
readings.passageScript:
“Have you ever explored a forest?” asked Nora.
“Yes, I have!” said Leo. “I walked under the tall trees last year.”
“No, I haven't,” said Amal. “I have only seen pictures of a forest.”

readings.title: From Coast to Forest
readings.setting: A coastal town: the school, the beach and coast, a forest path and a mountain trail
readings.passageScript:
It was the first week of term, and Teacher Yasmin had a big surprise.
“We are going on a nature trip!” she said. The whole class cheered.
“We will explore the coast, play on the beach, and hike a mountain trail.”
Then she smiled. “Have you ever explored a forest?”
“Yes, I have!” said Nora.
“No, I haven't,” said Amal. “I have only seen pictures.”
The next day, the class packed their bags.
“Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see,” said Teacher Yasmin.
“And remember — protect the planet. Take only pictures, leave only footprints.”
Their first stop was the beach. The sunshine was bright and the weather was hot.
They felt the soft, warm sand and saw tiny crabs running near the waves.
“This is the coast,” said the teacher. “Look at the water and the wide sky.”
Nora pointed at something shiny in the sand. “It is a rock — no, it is metal!”
“Touch it carefully,” said Teacher Yasmin. “It may be sharp.”
The children sat down and wrote a short poem together:
I see the waves,
I see the sand,
I hold a metal rock in my hand.
Later, the class travelled to the forest. The air felt cooler here.
“What is the temperature?” asked the teacher.
“Maybe twenty-two degrees,” said Amal.
“That is right,” she said. “The climate is different here — more shade and less wind.”
They walked along a path between the tall trees.
“Have you ever been inside a forest this big?” asked Nora.
“No, I haven't,” said Amal. “It feels like another world.”
Suddenly they saw something white and soft in the dirt, like snow.
“Is that ice?” asked Leo.
“No, it is not snow,” said the teacher. “But water here froze one very cold night.”
“Frozen water, so near our warm coast?” asked Amal, surprised.
“Yes,” the teacher smiled. “Even here, the weather can surprise us.”
They climbed higher and reached the mountain viewpoint.
“Look down,” said Teacher Yasmin. “You can see the beach and the forest from here.”
The wind blew gently across their faces.
“The air feels so fresh,” said Amal.
“That is natural energy,” said the teacher. “It comes from the sun and the wind.”
On the way back, they stopped beside a quiet pond.
“What is all this white foam?” asked Leo.
“It is a type of matter,” said Teacher Yasmin.
“Foam, water, rock and air are all matter, just in different forms.”
Back at school, the children shared what they had learned.
“I wrote about the sunshine,” said Nora. “It helps the plants grow near the coast.”
“I wrote about the forest,” said Amal. “I have seen animals and trees I never knew.”
The teacher asked, “Have you ever visited the beach or a mountain before today?”
Some children said yes. Others said no. Now they all had new memories.
Later, they made posters about nature. Amal's poster said:
Nature is our home.
It gives us water, trees, and air.
Let us take care — it is only fair.
That afternoon, Nora wrote in her notebook: “The planet has so much to see.
I have explored a forest, felt sunshine on the beach, and climbed a mountain.
Nature is not just green — it is exciting.”

comprehension.question: In “Our Wonderful Nature”, what does the sunshine do?
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence of the text states that the sunshine warms the land.

comprehension.question: Which words in “Our Wonderful Nature” show that the mountain is very high?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The words “High above, the mountain touches the clouds” show it.
comprehension.explanation: Touching the clouds is a picture of great height, so those words carry the meaning.

comprehension.question: In the poem, what three gifts does nature give us?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Nature gives us water, trees and air.
comprehension.explanation: Line two of the poem lists water, trees and air as the gifts.

comprehension.question: What does the last line of the poem ask every reader to do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It asks us to take care of nature and to share it with everyone.
comprehension.explanation: The closing line “Let's take care, and share it all” is a request to the reader.

comprehension.question: The speaker gives three instructions before the trip. What are they?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see.
comprehension.explanation: Each instruction begins with a verb, which is how the text signals a command.

comprehension.question: Which two places will the class explore tomorrow?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They will explore the coast and the forest.
comprehension.explanation: The first sentence names both places in the order coast, then forest.

comprehension.question: What question does Nora ask her friends, and what short answer does Leo give?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She asks “Have you ever explored a forest?” and Leo answers “Yes, I have!”
comprehension.explanation: The reply keeps the helping verb have, which is what makes it a short answer.

comprehension.question: Amal replies “No, I haven't.” What has she seen instead of a real forest?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She has only seen pictures of a forest.
comprehension.explanation: Amal's second sentence explains what she has seen, so the reply is not a simple no.

comprehension.question: Which three places did the class visit on their nature trip?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They visited the beach and coast, then the forest, then the mountain viewpoint.
comprehension.explanation: The story moves through the three places in that order, which is how the trip is built.

comprehension.question: What shiny thing did Nora find, and how did she describe it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She found metal, though she first thought it was a rock.
comprehension.explanation: Nora corrects herself in the same line, which shows she is still working the answer out.

comprehension.question: Why was the forest cooler than the beach, according to Teacher Yasmin?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The forest has more shade and less wind, so its climate is different.
comprehension.explanation: The teacher gives shade and wind as the reason, linking climate to a real cause.

comprehension.question: Amal says the forest “feels like another world”. What does this show?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She is amazed, because she had only seen forests in pictures.
comprehension.explanation: Her earlier line “I have only seen pictures” is the evidence behind the feeling.

grammar.title: The Present Perfect Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the present perfect for something that happened before but still matters today. When you say “I have explored a forest”, you tell people what your life has already included.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Formula: subject + have or has + past participle
Use have with I, you, we and they: “I have explored the coast.”
Use has with he, she and it: “She has seen the mountain.”
Most past participles end in -ed (explore → explored), but some are irregular (see → seen).
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “I have see a bird.” leaves the verb unfinished. The helper have always needs the past participle, so the sentence becomes “I have seen a bird.”
grammar.memoryTip: Picture the present perfect as a bridge from the past to today: have or has, then the thing you did.
grammar.practice: Write the present perfect with have or has and the correct past participle. | I ____ (explore) a cave. | They ____ (see) a shooting star. | She ____ (finish) her nature painting. | We ____ (visit) the forest.

grammar.title: Yes/No Questions with the Present Perfect
grammar.explanation: These questions ask about a whole life so far, without saying exactly when anything happened. They are a friendly way to start talking about places people know.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Formula: Have or Has + subject + past participle + extra words?
“Have you ever visited the beach?”
“Has she seen the sunshine after a storm?”
The word ever means “at any time in your life”.
grammar.commonMistake: Starting with “Has you ever been to the coast?” mixes the two helpers. The pronoun you takes have, so ask “Have you ever been to the coast?”
grammar.memoryTip: Have belongs with I, you, we and they. Has belongs with he, she and it. For a question, the helper jumps to the front.
grammar.practice: Fill in have or has, then the past participle in brackets. | ______ you ever ____ (go) to a mountain? | ______ she ____ (see) that forest before? | ______ they ____ (explore) the beach yet?

grammar.title: Short Answers
grammar.explanation: Short answers reply quickly instead of repeating the whole question. They keep a conversation moving and make you sound natural.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Take the helper from the question and add the subject.
“Have you explored a forest?” → “Yes, I have.” or “No, I haven't.”
“Has she seen the mountain?” → “Yes, she has.” or “No, she hasn't.”
grammar.commonMistake: “Yes, I explored a forest.” repeats far too much, and “Yes, I.” drops the helper altogether. The neat reply is “Yes, I have.”
grammar.memoryTip: Keep the helper and the answer can stand on its own two feet: yes or no, the subject, then have or has.
grammar.practice: Write a short answer for each question, choosing yes or no. | “Have you seen the sea?” → ______, I ______. | “Has your friend explored a cave?” → ______, he ______. | “Have they climbed the mountain?” → ______, they ______.

grammar.title: Irregular Past Participles
grammar.explanation: Most past participles simply add -ed, but a small group of very common verbs change their spelling instead. We meet these often after have and has.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
There is no single rule for these, so learn them by heart:
see → seen
be → been
freeze → frozen
go → gone, eat → eaten, write → written
grammar.commonMistake: “I have saw that lake.” borrows the past simple by accident. After have we need the participle, so write “I have seen that lake.”
grammar.memoryTip: Make flashcards with the base verb on one side and the past participle on the other, then test yourself every day.
grammar.practice: Fill each gap with seen, been, explored or frozen. | She has ____ to the mountains with her family. | The river has ____ over because of the cold. | I have ____ that big forest before. | They have ____ many caves on their trip.

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: An imperative tells someone to do something. We use imperatives for rules, recipes, safety notices and kind reminders such as “Protect the planet.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Begin with the base verb and no “to”: “Explore nature carefully.”
No subject is needed, because “you” is understood: “Wear a hat.”
For a negative, begin with Don't: “Don't drop litter.”
Add please to sound polite, or Let's to invite everybody.
grammar.commonMistake: “You to close the door.” and “To explore the forest.” both carry extra words. Start with the plain verb instead: “Close the door.”
grammar.memoryTip: An imperative works like a coach beside the pitch: the verb comes first and the message stays short.
grammar.practice: Write the imperative using the verb in brackets. | ____ (protect) the planet. | ____ (not drop) litter in the forest. | ____ (listen) to your teacher. | ____ (take) your water bottle on the trip.

grammar.title: Descriptive Phrases, Wh- Questions, Quantifiers and Reported Instructions
grammar.explanation: These four tools all sharpen a sentence. A descriptive phrase turns “a rock” into “a metal rock”, so the reader pictures it. A wh- question asks for exact information instead of a yes or no. A quantifier says how much or how many. A reported instruction carries somebody else's words on to a new listener.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Descriptive phrases: take a noun such as rock, add an adjective such as metal, and join them: “a metal rock on the beach.” Two adjectives take a comma between them: “a large, shiny rock.”
Wh- questions: choose the word that fits — who for people, what for things, where for places, when for time, why for reasons, how for the way. Add the verb, then finish the idea: “Where is the coast?” and “What is the weather like today?”
Quantifiers: countable nouns such as mountains and trees take many, a few or several: “Some mountains are very high.” Uncountable nouns such as sunshine and water take much, a little or a lot of: “A lot of sunshine is good for plants.” Some and a lot of suit both groups.
Reported instructions: use said to, or told someone to, then the base verb: “She told us to wear warm clothes.” For a negative, use not to: “He said not to run.” Change words such as me, us and my so the new sentence still makes sense.
grammar.commonMistake:
Describing phrases: “a small grey cat” runs two describing words together, and British writing puts a comma between them: “a small, grey cat.”
Wh- questions: “Where the coast?” has lost its verb along the way, so put the verb back: “Where is the coast?”
Quantifiers: “Much children play outside.” measures something we can count, and children can be counted: “Many children play outside.”
Reported instructions: “He said explore the forest.” has dropped the little word to, so write “He said to explore the forest.”
grammar.memoryTip: One mnemonic for each tool. Describing words add colour, so the reader sees the very thing you saw. Say the rhyme: “Who, what, where, when, why and how — ask them well, and you will learn now!” Can you count it? Choose many; can you only measure it? Choose much. Picture said to as a bridge that carries an instruction across to the next person.
grammar.practice: Part A — Add a describing word to each phrase, then rewrite the last sentence. Adjective bank: natural, sunny, frozen, hot, cold, fresh, steep, sandy, rocky. | a ____ mountain | the ____ weather | a ____ beach | Rewrite with a descriptive phrase: “The bird sings.” | Finish this with an adverb from carefully, gently, widely or deeply: “Explore nature ____.” | Part B — Write the correct wh- word in each gap. | ____ is the tallest mountain you know? | ____ is the weather like today? | ____ do you go to enjoy nature? | ____ is Earth special, do you think? | Part C — Choose the correct quantifier in each sentence. | There are ____ (many / much) trees in the forest. | We need ____ (some / much) water for the trip. | She saw ____ (a few / a little) birds on the coast. | There is ____ (a little / many) sand on my shoes. | Part D — Rewrite each sentence as a reported instruction. | “The teacher said, ‘Read the book.’” → The teacher said ____ the book. | “Dad told me, ‘Don't forget your hat!’” → Dad told me ____ my hat. | “She said, ‘Protect the planet.’” → She said ____ the planet.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe a Place in Nature
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three sentences about a place in nature:
“I like the ______ (beach / forest / mountain).”
“It is ______ and ______.” (describing words)
“There I can see ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the place? Did you use two describing words? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen with my adult helper while I describe a place in nature, then tell me whether my two describing words really matched the place.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Talk About the Weather
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Say three sentences about the weather where you live, then record your best try.
“Today the weather is ______.”
“The climate where I live is usually ______.”
“My favourite weather is ______ because ______.”
Check: did you use weather, climate, and one reason with because?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me to compare today's weather with our usual climate, and stop me kindly if I use the two words the wrong way round.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Have You Ever…?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Ask a question: “Have you ever explored a ______ (forest / beach / mountain)?”
The other person answers with a short answer: “Yes, I have!” or “No, I haven't.”
If they say yes, ask: “What did you see?” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play the “Have you ever…?” game with me while an adult is in the room, and tell me each time my short answer keeps the right helping verb.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Nature Word Interview
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose six words from this unit. Ask an adult one clear question about each word, and answer in a full sentence yourself.
Model: “What is the difference between the coast and the beach?”
Model: “Which planet do we live on?”
Record the interview, then listen for clear word endings.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Take turns with me, with an adult listening, and quiz me on six unit words until I can define each one in a full sentence.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Why We Protect the Planet
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Explain in three sentences why people should protect the planet. Give one reason with because, then answer two follow-up questions.
Model: “We must protect the planet because litter harms the crabs on the beach.”
Talk about it: Teacher Yasmin said, “Take only pictures, leave only footprints.” What does she mean, and why is it kind to nature?
Record your answer and check that your reason is clear.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me two follow-up questions about protecting the planet while an adult listens, and check that every answer of mine gives a reason with because.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Nature Talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose the place you would most like to visit: the beach, the forest or the mountain.
Plan and record a one-minute talk about it. Name the place, add three details about its weather, plants and animals, and close with one reason to visit.
Use a note card with only three words on it, rather than full sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Time my one-minute nature talk with an adult present, then tell me one thing that was clear and one thing to improve next time.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe the Nature Near You
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write four sentences about the weather and the nature near your home. Use the words weather and sunshine, and one place word: beach, forest or mountain.
writing.modelText: Our town sits close to the coast, so the weather is warm and windy. Bright sunshine wakes the market before eight o'clock. Behind our street, a green forest climbs the hill. I like the fresh air there after heavy rain.
writing.sentenceStarter: Near my home the weather is
writing.successCriteria: I used weather, sunshine and one place word; I wrote in complete sentences; I checked every capital letter and full stop.
writing.support: Say each sentence aloud first, then write it and read it back to an adult.
writing.extension: Draw the nature near your home, label three things in English, and add one sentence comparing today's weather with last week's.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Three Things You Have Done
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three present perfect sentences about nature. Begin them with “I have seen”, “I have explored” and “I have visited”.
writing.modelText: I have seen tiny crabs running across the wet sand. I have explored a forest path with my class and my teacher. I have visited the mountain viewpoint above our town.
writing.successCriteria: Each sentence uses have with a past participle; each one names a real place; I checked the spelling of every participle.
writing.support: Keep the participle list from Grammar 4 beside you while you write.
writing.extension: Add one more sentence about a friend, using has instead of have.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Your Own Nature Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write your own version of “Nature Is Our Home”. Change the middle lines to the parts of nature you love, then draw a picture beside your poem.
writing.modelText:
Nature is our home,
It gives us shade and rain,
The reef, the river, the mountain chain —
Let us take care, and share it all.
writing.successCriteria: My poem keeps four lines; two of them name real parts of nature; the last line asks the reader to take care.
writing.support: Copy the first line and the last line, then change only the middle two.
writing.extension: Try to make the second line and the fourth line rhyme with each other.

writing.title: Writing 4 — A Report on One Place in Nature
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence report about the beach, the forest or the mountain. Open with a topic sentence and join your ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: The forest behind our school is cool and quiet. Tall trees hold the shade all day, so the temperature stays low. Birds call from the branches, but the deer stay hidden. I like the forest because the air smells of rain and leaves.
writing.sentenceStarter: The place I want to describe is
writing.successCriteria: I began with a topic sentence; I joined ideas with and, but or because; I used at least four unit words.
writing.support: Plan three ideas as a short list before you write the paragraph.
writing.extension: Draw the place you described, then write one sentence under the picture telling readers how to protect it.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Fact File: Caring for Our Planet
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five true facts about caring for the planet. Number every fact and use unit words such as energy, litter and forest.
writing.modelText:
Caring for Our Planet
1. Our planet gives us clean water and fresh air.
2. Trees in a forest hold the soil in place.
3. Litter on the beach can harm the small crabs.
4. Wind and sunshine give us clean energy.
5. Every family can save water at home.
writing.successCriteria: Every fact is true and complete; each one is numbered; my heading matches the topic of the list.
writing.support: Reread “Our Wonderful Nature” and the story for ideas before you start.
writing.extension: Add a closing sentence that asks readers to do one of your five things.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one earlier piece of your writing. Improve three sentences, add two describing words, and explain one change you made.
writing.modelText: I changed “The beach was nice” to “The wide beach was warm and windy”. The new sentence gives the reader two describing words, so the picture is much clearer.
writing.successCriteria: Three sentences are rewritten; two describing words are added; my explanation names the improvement clearly.
writing.support: Read your first draft aloud and mark the sentences that sound dull.
writing.extension: Ask a partner which sentence improved most, and write down the reason.

activities.title: Activities 1 — True or False (Nature Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If a sentence is false, write the correct fact underneath.
1. A forest is a big area with many trees.
2. The coast is the land next to the sea.
3. Metal is a soft material that you can eat.
4. Earth is a planet.
activities.answerSummary: 1 True. 2 True. 3 False. 4 True. Sentence 3 is corrected with the real meaning of metal.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Match the Word to the Meaning
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its meaning and write the four pairs in your book.
1. climate — a) light and warmth from the sun
2. sunshine — b) the usual weather in a place
3. explore — c) to go and look at new places
4. temperature — d) how hot or cold something is

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks (have / has)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write have or has in each gap, then read the finished sentence aloud.
1. I ______ explored a forest.
2. She ______ seen the mountain.
3. We ______ visited the beach.
4. He ______ climbed a hill.
activities.answerSummary: The answers are have, has, have and has.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Care for the Planet
activities.instructionsAndItems: Write two imperatives that tell people how to care for nature. Begin each one with a verb, as in “Plant a tree.” Read them to an adult and explain why each one helps.
activities.answerSummary: Two verb-first commands, each with a spoken reason.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Nature Word Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort the fifteen unit words into three groups: weather words, place words and science words. Then write one sentence that explains the rule for each group.
activities.answerSummary: Three groups of words, each with a written rule.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Evidence Hunt in “From Coast to Forest”
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Find six details in the story that show the class was learning about nature. Copy each detail, then write what it teaches the reader.
Next, hunt for five unit words inside the story and read the whole sentence each one sits in aloud.
activities.answerSummary: Six copied details with explanations, plus five unit words read aloud in context.

assignments.title: Nature and the Environment portfolio: my nature trip
assignments.instructions:
Submit three pieces: Writing 2 (three present perfect sentences), Writing 5 (the Caring for Our Planet fact file), and a recording of Speaking 6 (your one-minute nature talk). Improve each piece once before you send it.
Marking guidance for the teacher: award up to 12 marks for Writing 2 using the Writing rubric, checking that every sentence pairs have or has with a real past participle. Award up to 10 marks for Writing 5, one mark for each true, numbered fact and up to five for accurate unit vocabulary and end punctuation. Award up to 10 marks for Speaking 6 using the Speaking rubric, listening for a named place, three details and a closing reason. Return one strength and one next step to every learner.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word “climate” mean?
quizzes.options: Everything in the world is made of it, and it takes up space. | The usual weather in a place over a long time. | A big ball in space, like Earth or Mars. | Power that helps things move or work.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The usual weather in a place over a long time.
quizzes.explanation: Climate describes the usual weather across many years, which is why the desert has a hot, dry climate.

quizzes.question: In the story, the sunshine was bright and the _____ was hot.
quizzes.explanation: Weather is what the sky and air are like on that one day, so it fits a single hot morning at the beach.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
quizzes.options: She has seen the mountain. | She have seen the mountain. | She has saw the mountain from the beach. | She seen the mountain.
quizzes.explanation: She takes has, and see becomes the past participle seen, so only the first sentence is complete and correct.

quizzes.question: Which is the correct short answer to “Have you explored a forest?”
quizzes.options: Yes, I explored. | Yes, I am. | Yes, I have. | Yes, I did explore a forest today.
quizzes.explanation: A short answer repeats the helping verb from the question, and that question begins with have.

quizzes.question: Which sentence is an imperative that tells someone what to do?
quizzes.options: The children protect the planet. | Protect the planet. | We are protecting the planet every day. | They will protect the planet.
quizzes.explanation: An imperative starts with the base verb and needs no subject, because the word you is understood.

quizzes.question: What is the past participle of the verb “freeze”?
quizzes.explanation: Freeze is irregular: froze is the past simple, but after have or has we need frozen.

quizzes.question: Which word completes the sentence: There are _____ trees in the forest.
quizzes.explanation: Trees can be counted one by one, and countable nouns take many rather than much.

quizzes.question: Which question word asks for a reason?
quizzes.options: Where | When | Why | How many times
quizzes.explanation: Why asks for the reason behind something, while where asks about place and when asks about time.

quizzes.question: In “From Coast to Forest”, what did Nora find shining in the sand?
quizzes.options: A piece of metal | A silver fish | A glass bottle | A small green leaf
quizzes.explanation: Nora first calls it a rock, then corrects herself and says it is metal.

quizzes.question: Which noun phrase uses describing words to paint a clear picture?
quizzes.options: a rock | a large, shiny rock | that rock over there | the rock
quizzes.explanation: Two adjectives with a comma between them give the reader size and surface, so the picture is much clearer.

liveSessions.title: Nature and the Environment: Trip launch and nature vocabulary
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the five Weather and Climate words.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min picture starter on the coast; 10 min teacher models climate, weather and temperature; 15 min word sort in pairs; 10 min sentence building with place words; 5 min exit question.
liveSessions.afterSession: Add the fifteen unit words to your word wall and tick the ones you can already use.

liveSessions.title: Nature and the Environment: Reading “Our Wonderful Nature” and the poem
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read “Our Wonderful Nature” once quietly, then once aloud to an adult.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min shared reading of the information text; 10 min modelled answers with text evidence; 15 min paired comprehension questions 1 to 4; 10 min poem read aloud in two voices; 5 min favourite line share.
liveSessions.afterSession: Write one sentence explaining which text you preferred and why.

liveSessions.title: Nature and the Environment: Present perfect workshop
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar lessons 1 and 4, including both practice sets.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval of have and has; 10 min teacher models the participle bridge; 15 min guided gap-fill from Activity 3; 10 min partner sentences about real places; 5 min error hunt on the board.
liveSessions.afterSession: Draft the three present perfect sentences for Writing 2.

liveSessions.title: Nature and the Environment: “Have you ever…?” speaking circle
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar lessons 2 and 3, and rehearse two short answers.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min pronunciation of temperature and climate; 10 min teacher models the question and the short answer; 15 min speaking circle with swapped turns; 10 min recording in pairs; 5 min feedback on helping verbs.
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 3, then tell an adult about a place that once felt new and exciting to you.

liveSessions.title: Nature and the Environment: Describing phrases and writing conference
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar 6 Part A on describing phrases, and bring your Writing 1 draft.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min adjective warm-up on the story rock; 10 min teacher models a noun phrase with two adjectives; 15 min writing conference in threes; 10 min revision time; 5 min share one improved sentence.
liveSessions.afterSession: Improve Writing 4 using the describing phrases from the conference.

liveSessions.title: Nature and the Environment: Care for the planet, quiz and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar 5, then Grammar 6 Parts C and D, and draft two imperatives.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min imperative chant; 10 min teacher models a reported instruction; 15 min poster planning for the fact file; 10 min unit quiz; 5 min self-assessment review.
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the self-assessment and submit the unit portfolio.

teacherNotes.note:
Model the present perfect aloud before any independent work, and check the meaning of climate, matter and energy with real objects or pictures. An adult stays beside the child for every AI-tutor task and every recording; no learner uses the device alone, and no recording leaves the family device. Give feedback on complete sentences, quoted text evidence and precise nature vocabulary rather than on handwriting.
Suggested daily routine, about 30 to 45 minutes: review the word wall and say the weather aloud in the morning; do one grammar lesson or one activity in the afternoon; practise speaking and read part of the story in the evening; bring recordings and writing to the teacher session twice a week.
Bridge to Unit 8: Unit 8 covers numbers, shapes and measurement, so ask learners to start counting trees and noticing the shape of a leaf or a mountain.

AI-tutor prompts from the source pack, grouped by section, for use with an adult present.
Reading and listening: “Can you read the nature poem with me and explain it?” · “Can you play a ‘Have you ever…?’ listening game with me?”
Speaking: “Can you help me practise describing a place in nature?” · “How do I pronounce ‘temperature’ and ‘climate’?”
Writing: “Can you check if my present-perfect sentences are correct?” · “Can you help me make my nature poem rhyme?”
Vocabulary: “What is the difference between weather and climate?” · “What is the difference between the coast and the beach?” · “Can you give me more examples of matter?” · “What are some ways to save energy at home?”
Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?” · “Why do we say ‘she has’ but ‘I have’?” · “Can you give me five more present-perfect questions about nature?”
Story: “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1 True. 2 True. 3 False — metal is a hard, shiny material used to make things, and it is never food. 4 True, Earth is the planet we live on.
1–b climate, 2–a sunshine, 3–c explore, 4–d temperature. Ask the learner to say each meaning back as a full sentence.
1 have. 2 has. 3 have. 4 has. Point out that he, she and it always take has, and everything else takes have.
Accept any two commands that begin with a base verb, such as “Turn off the tap.” or “Pick up your litter.” Underline the verb the learner chose and check the full stop.

Weather: climate, weather, temperature, sunshine, froze. Places: mountain, forest, beach, coast, nature, explore. Science: energy, matter, metal, planet. Accept explore in the place group because it names what we do there.

Strong choices include the metal in the sand, the twenty-two degrees in the forest, the water that froze, the wind called natural energy, the foam described as matter, and the poster about caring for nature.
The usual weather in a place over a long time. The other three options define matter, planet and energy.
weather. Climate is wrong because one hot day is weather, not the pattern of many years.
She has seen the mountain. Option 2 uses the wrong helper, option 3 uses the past simple, option 4 has no helper at all.
Yes, I have. A short answer keeps the helper from the question and drops everything else.
Protect the planet. The other three are statements with a subject in front of the verb.
frozen. Froze is the past simple form, freezed is not a word, and freezing is the -ing form.
many. Much, a little and less all belong with things we measure instead of count.
Why. Where asks about place, when asks about time, and how many times asks about number.
A piece of metal. The story shows Nora correcting herself: “It is a rock — no, it is metal!”
a large, shiny rock. The other phrases point at the rock but never describe how it looks.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and describe the beach, the coast, the forest and the mountain.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain how the weather today is different from our climate.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what I have done using have or has with the right past participle.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask a “Have you ever…?” question and give a correct short answer.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can add describing words to make my writing clearer.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can write instructions that tell people how to care for our planet.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
